w:r.w:t: Version 1.0 · March 2026 · Standalone Build — No Existing Codebase Required

that joins a scheduled video meeting in place of a human interviewer. The bot is represented by a talking AI avatar, asks intelligent and adaptive questions based on the candidate's resume and the job description, listens to the candidate's spoken answers, evaluates them in real time, and produces a structured interview report at the end.
When a recruiter at Jobsify schedules an AI interview, the candidate receives a meeting link. At the scheduled time, the
AI avatar bot joins the call as a participant

. The candidate sees a realistic animated interviewer on screen. The interview proceeds naturally — the avatar speaks, listens, asks follow-up questions, and wraps up professionally. The recruiter can monitor live or review the full report afterward.
A candidate should not need special software — the interview happens in a standard video call (Google Meet, Zoom, or a custom WebRTC room) and feels like talking to a real interviewer.
Controls interview lifecycle: start, question loop, pauses, wrap-up. Coordinates all other modules
Joins a video call as a participant; injects avatar video + audio feed; captures candidate audio
Converts candidate speech to text (STT) in real time; converts AI text to speech (TTS) for the avatar
Renders a talking head video synchronized with TTS audio and streams it into the call
Generates the next question using LLM based on job description, resume, interview plan, and prior answers
Scores each candidate answer for relevance, depth, communication, and red flags
Produces a structured post-interview PDF/JSON report for the recruiter
The candidate should have a low-friction experience. No app download. No account required. One meeting link.
The avatar should not robotically read from a fixed list. Questions should respond to what the candidate just said (follow-up on interesting points, skip topics they already answered).
At the start of every interview, the avatar must clearly state: 'I am an AI interviewer. This interview is being recorded and evaluated.' Non-negotiable.
If any module fails (TTS fails, STT drops, LLM times out), the session continues with safe fallbacks — not a crash.
A recruiter dashboard lets humans monitor live sessions, intervene, extend time, or terminate early.

You are NOT building a job board, a resume parser, a scheduling calendar, or a billing system. Those are Jobsify's responsibility. Your API will receive a pre-scheduled session payload and must handle everything from join to report.
│ │ ├── config.py # All env vars via Pydantic settings
│ │ ├── speech/ # M3: Deepgram STT + ElevenLabs TTS
Async REST + WebSocket server. Use lifespan context manager for startup/shutdown of audio streams.
Bot joins as a participant. Free tier allows 10,000 participant-minutes/month — sufficient for dev.
Real-time streaming transcription via WebSocket. 30ms latency. Supports speaker diarization.
Most natural-sounding TTS. Use a consistent voice ID per avatar persona. Stream audio chunks.
Takes TTS audio → renders a talking head video in real time. Free tier: 5 min/month (dev only).
Question generation (M5) + answer evaluation (M6). Use structured output (JSON mode).
Recruiter dashboard only. No React for candidate — they use their own video client.
Push transcript, scores, session state to recruiter dashboard in real time.
Sessions, transcripts, scores, reports. Use Alembic for migrations.
Store recorded audio, generated reports (PDF), avatar assets.
Async report generation, email delivery. Do not block the API thread.
Playwright for dashboard E2E tests. pytest for all backend logic.
All services including MinIO, Redis, PostgreSQL in one compose file.

All recommended services have free or generous dev tiers. Daily.co (free), Deepgram ($200 free credit), ElevenLabs (10,000 chars/month free), D-ID ($5 free credit), OpenAI (pay-as-you-go, avg $0.02/interview). Budget: under $10/month during development.
M1 is the brain of the system. It manages the interview as a finite state machine (FSM) and coordinates all other modules. It does not do speech, video, or AI itself — it only orchestrates.
Bot joins meeting room. Avatar visible but silent. Wait for candidate to join.
Avatar plays mandatory AI disclosure + greeting. Reads candidate name and job title from session config.
Intro complete OR prior answer received
M5 generates next question. M3 TTS speaks it. M4 animates avatar. Start answer timeout timer.
M2 begins capturing candidate audio. M3 STT streams transcript. Detect silence (> 3 seconds) as end-of-answer signal.
M6 evaluates the transcript. Score stored. M1 decides: follow-up, next topic, or wrap-up.
Evaluator signals low depth or interesting thread
M5 generates a follow-up question targeting the gap. Returns to ASKING.
All topics covered OR max_duration reached
Avatar delivers closing script. Thanks candidate. Announces report delivery timeline.
Bot leaves call. M7 triggered async to generate report. Session state = COMPLETE.
Avatar speaks: 'I experienced a technical issue. Please contact support.' Bot leaves gracefully.
# Get session status (poll or use WebSocket)
# WebSocket: real-time feed for recruiter dashboard
Server pushes: { event, payload } where event is one of:
M2 joins a video meeting as a named participant (e.g., 'Alex — AI Interviewer'), injects the avatar's video/audio into the call, and captures the candidate's raw audio stream for STT processing.
Native SDK. Full A/V control. Create a Daily room via API for each session. Best for development.
Third-party bot service. Recall joins as a participant, forwards audio. $0.10/min. Use for production.
Same as Google Meet path. Recall supports Zoom, Teams, Meet under one API.
For Jobsify's own video infrastructure post-integration.
Build and demo using Daily.co rooms. The integration with Google Meet/Zoom via Recall.ai is architecturally identical — just swap the bot client. Document both paths.
# Feed TTS audio into the call as the bot's microphone
def on_participant_audio(self, participant_id: str, audio_data: bytes):
The STT pipeline must run in real time with low latency. The transcript appears on the recruiter dashboard as the candidate speaks, and triggers the evaluator when silence is detected.
interim_results=True, # show partial transcripts in real time
utterance_end_ms=3000, # 3 seconds silence = end of utterance
The TTS pipeline converts the question text to audio, streams it to M4 (for lip sync) and to M2 (for audio injection into the call). Always stream — never wait for the full audio to generate.
async def speak(text: str, voice_id: str) -> AsyncGenerator[bytes, None]:
# Fan out: send to avatar engine AND meeting bot simultaneously
# These are internal service calls, not public REST endpoints
{ "text": "Tell me about a time you led a complex project.",
"voice_preset": "alex", // maps to a configured ElevenLabs voice_id
# STT: get live transcript for a session

M4 renders a realistic talking-head video from TTS audio and streams it into the video call. The avatar must have natural facial movements synchronized to speech. The visual quality should not distract the candidate.
async def create_stream(self, presenter_id: str) -> str:
async def send_audio_chunk(self, stream_id: str, audio_b64: str):
# Push TTS audio; D-ID renders lip-synced video frames
async def get_video_stream_url(self, stream_id: str) -> str:
# WebRTC URL to inject into Daily.co call
Define at least 3 avatar personas. Each persona has a name, a presenter image (hosted URL), and a linked ElevenLabs voice ID. Personas should feel diverse and professional.
Default. Suitable for technical and general interviews.
If D-ID streaming fails, fall back to a static avatar image (PNG) with animated audio waveform overlay. The interview must continue — never crash on avatar failure.

M5 generates the next interview question at each ASKING state. Questions must be relevant to the job, informed by the candidate's background, and adaptive to what was already said. This is the most intellectually complex module.
Job description, required skills, candidate resume summary, interview config (topics, max questions, time elapsed), full transcript so far.
Based on which topics remain uncovered (track covered topics in session state) and interview stage (early = rapport + background; mid = technical; late = behavioural + culture fit).
Pass the full context to GPT-4o-mini with a structured prompt. Request JSON output with the question, rationale, expected answer keywords, and follow-up triggers.
Check: question is in English, is a question (ends with '?'), is not a duplicate of previous questions (cosine similarity check). Retry once on failure.
Append question to session.questions list with timestamp and topic tag.
SYSTEM: You are an expert technical interviewer for {company}. Your goal is to assess
whether the candidate is genuinely qualified for the role of {job_title}.
You ask ONE question at a time. Questions are specific, relevant, and open-ended.
Never ask closed yes/no questions. Never repeat a topic already covered.
Generate the next question. Return JSON ONLY:
"follow_up_trigger": "if candidate mentions X, follow up on Y"
Always maintain a local question bank as a fallback for when the LLM is unavailable or too slow. The bank should have at least 50 questions organized by topic and seniority level.
"What programming languages are you most comfortable with, and why?",
"Describe a project you built from scratch — what was the hardest part?"
"Describe a system you designed. What trade-offs did you make?",
"How do you approach technical debt in a fast-moving codebase?"
M6 scores each candidate answer immediately after the answer is transcribed. Scores are visible to the recruiter on the live dashboard and stored for the final report.
Does the answer address what was asked? Penalise tangents and non-answers.
Is the answer substantive? Look for: specific examples, numbers, outcomes. Penalise vague generalities.
For technical questions: are facts, terminology, and approaches correct? Use expected_keywords from M5 as a weak signal.
Is the answer clear and structured? STAR format (Situation, Task, Action, Result) scores max. Rambling or incoherent answers score low.
Detect: contradicts resume, admits critical skill gap, unprofessional language, extremely short answer (< 20 words for non-trivial question).
SYSTEM: You are an expert interview evaluator. Score candidate answers precisely.
Do not be lenient. Return structured JSON only.
"red_flags": [], // array of strings, empty if none
"brief_feedback": "1-2 sentence summary for recruiter (not shown to candidate)",
"followup_reason": "optional: what gap to probe in the follow-up question"
Evaluation must complete within 5 seconds. If the LLM is slow, return a partial score with is_preliminary=true and update it when the full result arrives. Never block the next question.

M7 generates a structured post-interview report for the recruiter. The report is generated asynchronously after the session ends (using a Celery task). It must be ready within 3 minutes of session end.
Name, job applied for, interview date, duration, avatar persona used.
Weighted aggregate of all answer scores. Display as a number (0–100) and a tier: Exceptional (90+), Strong (75–89), Adequate (60–74), Weak (45–59), Not Recommended (<45).
Average score per topic area (technical, behavioural, etc.) as a mini scorecard.
Each question, the answer transcript, score breakdown, and recruiter feedback note.
Top 3 strengths inferred from high-scoring answers. Cite specific quotes from the transcript.
Top 3 concerns. Include any red flags detected. Cite evidence.
One of: Advance to Next Round / Hold for Comparison / Reject with Feedback / Reject.
Time-stamped full interview transcript with speaker labels (Alex / Candidate).
"strengths": ["Strong Python depth with quantified production experience", ...],
Form: candidate name + email, job title, meeting platform, date/time, avatar persona, interview config (topics, duration). On submit: call POST /api/v1/sessions. Show generated meeting link to copy.
Table of all sessions: candidate name, job title, status (SCHEDULED / LIVE / COMPLETE), date, overall score (if complete). Click row to open Live Monitor or Report.

Real-time view via WebSocket. Shows: current FSM state, live transcript feed, current question, per-answer scores as they appear, elapsed time, avatar status. Recruiter override buttons: Pause, Skip Question, End Interview, Extend 5 min.
Full report view as described in M7. PDF export button. Option to add recruiter notes. Option to mark recommendation (can override AI recommendation).
{ event: string, payload: object, session_id: string, timestamp: string }
// Events the server sends to the dashboard:
{ event: 'transcript_final', payload: { text, is_final: true, utterance_id } }
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
meeting_type VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL, -- daily | google_meet | zoom
config JSONB NOT NULL, -- { max_duration_minutes, topics, ... }
recommendation VARCHAR(30), -- advance | hold | reject | reject_feedback
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
session_id UUID REFERENCES sessions(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
question_id UUID REFERENCES questions(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
session_id UUID REFERENCES sessions(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
session_id UUID REFERENCES sessions(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
ts_offset_ms INTEGER NOT NULL, -- milliseconds from session start
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
session_id UUID UNIQUE REFERENCES sessions(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'generating', -- generating | ready | failed
data JSONB, -- full report JSON as described in M7

Real-time audio/video systems are notoriously hard to test. You must test at multiple levels: pure logic (no mocks needed), service-level (mock external APIs), and integration (with real or simulated audio).
All state transitions: WAITING→INTRO, ASKING→LISTENING, EVALUATING→FOLLOWUP etc. Test all error transitions too.
M5 with a mocked OpenAI client. Verify: JSON output shape, no duplicate questions, topic coverage logic, fallback to question bank when LLM mocked to fail.
M6 with fixture transcripts (strong answer, weak answer, off-topic answer, very short answer). Verify scores are within expected ranges.
M7 with a complete fixture session (10 questions + answers). Verify all sections present, recommendation logic correct, PDF generates without error.
Small smoke tests against real Deepgram and ElevenLabs APIs. Keep in a separate 'integration' test directory. Only run in CI with a flag.
Connect to WS, simulate session events, assert dashboard receives correct event payloads in order.
Schedule a session, view the session list, open live monitor, verify WebSocket events render, view a completed report.
Build a special test mode for running a complete simulated interview without any real A/V. This is essential for CI and for demos.
"I led the backend team for 3 years using Python and FastAPI...",
"The biggest challenge was database performance at scale..."
# Server plays back the simulated answers as if spoken by the candidate
# No real audio/video — all STT/TTS/Avatar calls are mocked
# Returns complete session + report when done
# Essential for automated testing and demos
M1 (Session Orchestrator / FSM) + M2 (Meeting Bot) + PostgreSQL schema + WebSocket hub
M3 (STT + TTS) + M4 (Avatar Engine) + audio pipeline + D-ID integration
M5 (Question Engine) + M6 (Evaluator) + M7 (Report) + Recruiter Dashboard (React)

Docker Compose with FastAPI + PostgreSQL + Redis running. DB schema created. Session FSM skeleton with all states defined (no real integrations yet). GitHub repo with CI skeleton. Hello-world WebSocket endpoint.

M3: Deepgram STT streaming working — can transcribe live microphone input. ElevenLabs TTS working — can speak a text string. Audio test script: type a sentence, hear it spoken, speak a reply, see it transcribed.

M2: Daily.co room creation via API. Bot joins room with audio/video. Bot can inject TTS audio as its microphone. Bot can receive candidate audio and forward to STT. End-to-end: bot speaks, human speaks, transcript appears.
M4: D-ID streaming session created. TTS audio forwarded to D-ID. Talking head video stream injected into Daily.co call. Fallback static avatar with waveform working.
M5: Question engine with LLM + question bank fallback. M6: Answer evaluator with LLM. Full simulated interview works: bot asks questions, processes answers, produces per-answer scores.
M1 FSM drives a full interview from WAITING to ENDED. M7 report generates as Celery task. All 7 APIs functional. Simulated interview mode complete and tested.
React recruiter dashboard: Schedule, Session List, Live Monitor (WebSocket), Report view with PDF export. All pages connected to real backend.
Load testing (simulate 5 concurrent interviews). All tests passing (unit + integration + E2E). Handoff document. Demo recording. Code freeze.
Backend + frontend. Type hints + docstrings. No debug prints. No hardcoded secrets. Ruff linting passes.
docker-compose up starts everything. A fresh developer can run a simulated interview within 5 minutes. Includes seed data (1 candidate, 1 job, 1 completed session + report).
5-minute screen recording of a full interview: bot joins, greets, asks 4+ questions, wraps up. Show recruiter dashboard live monitor and final report.
pytest passes with 0 failures. Coverage > 75% for backend/app/. Playwright E2E tests for dashboard. Simulated interview mode tested end-to-end.
Swagger UI at /api/docs. All 12+ endpoints documented with examples. Postman collection exported.
data/question_bank.json with 60+ questions across 4 topics and 3 seniority levels.
2–5 page PDF: how to run, how to extend avatar personas, how Jobsify will integrate, known limitations, LLM cost estimates per 1,000 interviews.
Full interview runs end-to-end (bot joins → asks → listens → scores → reports)
Demo video shows a real interview without crashes
Manual review of 10 generated questions in demo
Evaluators score 5 sample answers; compare to LLM scores
Clean FSM, separation of modules, type hints, no God classes
pytest passes, coverage > 75%, GitHub Actions running
E2E tests pass; live monitor shows real-time updates
Fresh team member can run demo in < 10 minutes from README
You do not implement any of this. This section describes what Jobsify engineering will do after the internship to connect your system.
When a recruiter clicks 'Schedule AI Interview' in Jobsify, Jobsify's backend will call POST /api/v1/sessions with candidate_id and job_id from its own database.
Jobsify will also call the Scoring Engine (the other intern project!) to get the candidate's skill list and resume summary, then inject it into the session config.
After the interview, Jobsify will call GET /api/v1/sessions/{id}/report and store the JSON in its own Firestore database.
Jobsify's email system will handle meeting link delivery to candidates. Your service just needs to expose the meeting URL in the session response.

All team members sign up for: Daily.co (free), Deepgram (free $200 credit), ElevenLabs (free tier), D-ID (free $5 credit), OpenAI (pay-as-you-go). Store all API keys in a shared .env — never commit to git.
Create GitHub repo. Add all members. Branch protection on main. GitHub Actions skeleton: ruff + mypy + pytest on every PR.
docker-compose.yml with: FastAPI, PostgreSQL, Redis, MinIO, Celery worker. Confirm docker-compose up starts cleanly.
Implement the Session FSM with all 9 states and transitions defined. No real integrations yet — just state objects and placeholder handlers. This unblocks Team A.
All three sub-teams agree on internal service interfaces before writing service code. Write the Pydantic models for all request/response schemas. Commit and do not change without team discussion.
Team B: write a script that speaks 'Hello, I am Alex, your AI interviewer today' using ElevenLabs and transcribes it back with Deepgram. This validates both APIs are working.
Create initial Alembic migration with all tables. Run and verify with psql. Write fixture data loader.
Create a Daily.co room via API, join it from a browser, verify bot can join as second participant. This is the riskiest integration — de-risk it early.

The audio pipeline (M2 + M3) is the highest-risk part. Prioritise getting audio flowing end-to-end in Week 1, even if the question engine and avatar are mocked. Everything else is easier once audio works.
— Async task queue for report generation
Q: Does the candidate need to download anything?

No. They receive a standard meeting link (Daily.co room URL in development, Google Meet / Zoom in production). They join exactly as they would any normal video call. The bot appears as another participant.
Q: What if the candidate's internet drops mid-interview?

M1 FSM should transition to a PAUSED state when the candidate's participant track is lost (Daily.co fires a participant-left event). Wait up to 3 minutes for reconnect. If no reconnect, transition to ENDED with reason='candidate_disconnected'. The partial report should still be generated.
Q: What if the D-ID quota runs out?
This is handled by the avatar fallback rule in M4. A static profile image with an animated audio waveform overlay must always be the fallback. The interview continues without interruption.
Q: Can the LLM generate inappropriate questions?

This risk must be mitigated by your prompt design. The prompt must explicitly forbid questions about: age, gender, marital status, religion, national origin, disability (all protected characteristics). Add a validation step: run the generated question through a keyword blocklist before speaking it. If blocked, pull from the question bank instead.
Q: How many questions per interview?
Configurable per session via max_questions (default 10, max 20). The FSM tracks covered topics and wraps up gracefully when all topics are covered or the time limit is hit — whichever comes first.
Q: Do we need a mobile-friendly recruiter dashboard?
Responsive layout is expected (Tailwind makes this easy) but deep mobile optimization is not required for the internship. Prioritise desktop-first.
Q: Can we swap D-ID for another avatar provider?
Yes. HeyGen is a strong alternative. Synthesia is higher quality but more expensive. The M4 module should be written with an abstract
class so the provider can be swapped by changing an env var.
Jobsify.ai · AI Interview Avatar System · Intern Requirements v1.0 · Confidential
Questions? Contact the Jobsify Engineering Team.
